title: Question 3
stem: A 32 year old with no prenatal care presents with premature rupture of membranes and delivers an infant at approximately 31 weeks with Apgars of 5 and 6 at 1 and 5 minutes. At delivery, a maternal fever of 101.5 °F and foul smelling amniotic fluid are identified. The placental surface and fetal membranes are green tinged and opaque. The umbilical cord reveals the features seen in the submitted image (PAS stain). Which of the following gross features would most likely be found on careful macroscopic examination of the umbilical cord?
teaching_point: . Peripheral white plaque(s). While often no macroscopic lesions are seen in cases of mild-moderate umbilical vasculitis or funisitis, gross findings are evident in some circumstances. A common gross finding with funisitis is the presence of peripheral white, creamy or yellowish plaques / lesions, which are often due to infection by Candida species (which is present in the above image). Additionally, white concentric rings may be appreciated around umbilical vessels. This finding is usually associated with necrotizing funisitis and represents the accumulation of dying cells, cellular debris and associated immune complexes that aggregate as arcs around vessels (analogous to the precipitin arcs seen in an Ouchterlony immunodiffusion assay). Umbilical vasculitis and funisitis

title: Question 12
stem: A 32 year old woman was diagnosed with a placental site trophoblastic tumor. She had a missed abortion 2 years prior to diagnosis. Her serum hCG before surgery was 550 mIU/mL. Imaging did not identify any metastatic foci. The patient underwent a total hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. The tumor was found to measure 6.5 cm in greatest dimension with focal extension into the right ovary. What is her FIGO stage and prognostic score?
teaching_point: FIGO stage II; risk score 7. Involvement of the ovary and the lack of metastasis makes this a pT2 M0 tumor, which corresponds to FIGO stage II. The patient is < 40 years old (score 0) with an antecedent abortion (score 1) which occurred 2 years prior (score 4). Her pretreatment hCG is below 1,000 IU/mL (score 0) and her largest tumor size is 6.5 cm (score 2). She has no metastatic disease or involvement beyond the reproductive organs (score 0 + 0) and no prior treatment (score 0). Taken together, her prognostic score is 0 + 1 + 4 + 0 + 2 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 7. title: Question 47
stem: How is eosinophilic / T cell chorionic vasculitis diagnosed?
teaching_point: . Histological examination of the placenta. At present, there are no recognized laboratory, radiological or macroscopic features that indicate the presence of eosinophilic / T cell chorionic vasculitis. Diagnosis can only be made at present by histological identification of a mixed T cell, eosinophilic and histiocytic infiltrate within the vessel wall of ≥ 1 chorionic, umbilical or stem villous vessel in the absence associated neutrophil polymorphs. macroscopic examination involves visually inspecting the placenta for gross abnormalities, such as size, shape and visible lesions or infarctions. However, eosinophilic / T cell vasculitis requires microscopic evaluation to identify specific cellular infiltrates and detailed vascular changes that cannot be seen with the naked eye. while a CBC can provide information about the overall health of the mother, including white blood cell counts and the presence of eosinophilia (an increased number of eosinophils), it does not provide direct information about the cellular composition or specific inflammatory processes within the placenta. Diagnosing eosinophilic / T cell chorionic vasculitis requires examination of placental tissue. ultrasound scans are used to assess the placenta and fetus's physical and functional status, such as placental location, blood flow and fetal growth. While they can identify some placental abnormalities, they cannot detect specific cellular infiltrates or the detailed histopathological changes characteristic of eosinophilic / T cell vasculitis. Ultrasound is not capable of providing the microscopic detail necessary for this diagnosis. Eosinophilic / T cell vasculitis

title: Question 67
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis of this well circumscribed placental mass near the chorionic plate?
teaching_point: . Chorangioma. Chorangiomas are likely hamartomas of the placental stem villi forming vascular masses nearly always peripheral and adjacent to the chorionic plate. They are well demarcated, firm masses when grossly identifiable and most are incidental and microscopic. Leiomyomas from the uterus can be adherent to the basal plate of the placenta and are extremely rare masses, extraneous to the placental parenchyma. Placental infarcts are typically firm and gritty triangular or rectangular masses at the maternal floor (not the chorionic plate). Intervillous thrombi are laminated irregularly shaped red or white soft masses anywhere in the placenta. Placental associated inflammatory myofibroblastic tumors are also unusual extrinsic masses that can be attached to the placenta basal plate or membranes. Chorangioma

title: Question 68
stem: What are complications associated with chorangioma?
teaching_point: . Fetal and maternal hydrops. Vascular steal and heart failure due to fetal perfusion of a large chorangioma can lead to fetal hydrops. Maternal mirror syndrome is a rare but serious complication of hydrops fetalis. Malignant transformation does not occur in chorangiomas; if malignancy occurs, the diagnosis is likely an intraplacental choriocarcinoma, which can have associated hypervascular villi. Visceral hemangiomas in the mother are not associated with chorangiomas but fetal / neonatal hemangiomas of the skin have been reported. Chorangiomas rarely reach a size which could complicate delivery. Chorangioma
